title: Deck Cost Calculator
subtitle: 7 questions, real numbers from Bozeman deck projects. Not national averages — actual Gallatin Valley material and labor costs.

steps.question: What kind of deck project is this?
steps.subtitle: This sets the baseline for everything else.
steps.why: A brand-new deck build means foundation, framing, and decking from scratch. A rebuild reuses some existing structure. A repair might just be boards and rails. This one question changes the estimate by thousands.

options.label: New deck build
options.desc: No existing deck — building from the ground up

options.label: Full rebuild
options.desc: Tear off the old deck and start fresh

options.label: Partial rebuild
options.desc: Keep the frame, replace decking and rails

options.label: Repair or refresh
options.desc: Fix specific issues — boards, stairs, rails

steps.question: How big is the deck?
steps.subtitle: A rough estimate works — we'll measure precisely on-site.
steps.why: Materials and labor both scale with square footage. Lumber, fasteners, and joist count are all driven by size. Even a rough number gets us in the right ballpark — Bozeman lumber prices are what make this accurate, not national averages.

options.label: Small
options.desc: Under 150 sq ft (8×16, entry landing, small patio)

options.label: Medium
options.desc: 150–300 sq ft (12×20, standard back deck)

options.label: Large
options.desc: 300–500 sq ft (16×24, entertaining deck)

options.label: Extra large
options.desc: 500+ sq ft (multi-level, wraparound, or custom)

steps.question: How high off the ground?
steps.subtitle: This affects foundation, railing requirements, and permits.
steps.why: In Gallatin County, decks over 30 inches above grade require railings and typically need a building permit. Ground-level decks skip both — saving $1,500–4,000 in railings alone. Height also determines whether you need concrete footings or can use deck blocks.

options.label: Ground level
options.desc: Under 12 inches — floating or on blocks

options.label: Low profile
options.desc: 12–30 inches — no railing required

options.label: Standard height
options.desc: 30–60 inches — railing required

options.label: Elevated
options.desc: 5+ feet — second story, walkout, or hillside

steps.question: What material for the decking?
steps.subtitle: This is your biggest material cost decision.
steps.why: Pressure-treated lumber runs $2–3 per linear foot in Bozeman — solid and affordable, but needs maintenance every few years. Cedar is beautiful but pricier. Composite (Trex, TimberTech) costs 2–3× more upfront but lasts decades with zero staining. Material choice alone can swing a deck project by $3,000–8,000.

options.label: Pressure-treated
options.desc: Affordable, proven — needs staining every 2–3 years

options.label: Cedar
options.desc: Beautiful grain, natural weather resistance

options.label: Composite (mid)
options.desc: Trex Select, TimberTech Edge — low maintenance

options.label: Composite (premium)
options.desc: Trex Transcend, TimberTech Vintage — top tier

steps.question: What about railings?
steps.subtitle: Required if your deck is over 30" above grade.
steps.why: Wood railings are the budget option at $30–50 per linear foot installed. Cable rail and metal balusters look sharp but cost 2–3× more. Glass panels are premium at $80–150+ per foot. If your deck is under 30 inches, you might skip them entirely — or add a simple cap rail for looks.

options.label: No railing
options.desc: Ground-level or low deck — not required

options.label: Wood railing
options.desc: PT or cedar posts and balusters — classic look

options.label: Metal or cable
options.desc: Aluminum balusters or cable rail — modern and clean

options.label: Glass panels
options.desc: Tempered glass — unobstructed views, premium cost

steps.question: Any extras?
steps.subtitle: Select everything that applies. These are the common add-ons we see in Bozeman.
steps.why: Each of these is a standalone scope item with its own materials and labor. Built-in benches and planters add $800–2,000 each. Lighting runs $600–2,000 depending on complexity. A pergola can add $3,000–8,000. We price these from our actual Bozeman project costs.

steps.question: When are you thinking?
steps.subtitle: Helps us plan the schedule — Bozeman deck season books up fast.
steps.why: Montana's build season for outdoor work is roughly May through October. Bozeman contractors book up quickly in spring — if you're targeting summer, starting the conversation now gives you the best shot at getting on the schedule.

options.label: As soon as possible
options.desc: Ready to move — let's get on the schedule

options.label: 1–3 months
options.desc: Planning ahead, want to get numbers

options.label: 3–6 months
options.desc: Thinking about next season

options.label: Just exploring
options.desc: Curious what a deck costs in Bozeman

project_type.new_build: New builds in the Gallatin Valley typically require permits and engineered footings due to frost depth.
project_type.full_rebuild: Demo and haul-off of the existing deck is included — we handle everything.
project_type.partial_rebuild: Reusing the existing frame saves significant cost if the structure is sound.
project_type.repair_refresh: Targeted repairs are the most cost-effective way to extend your deck's life.

deck_size.small: Smaller decks are straightforward builds — typically 2–4 days of construction.
deck_size.medium: The most common size we build in Bozeman — good balance of space and cost.
deck_size.large: Larger footprints need more foundation points and material — but cost per square foot drops.
deck_size.xl: Multi-level or wraparound decks involve more complex framing and often need engineered plans.

deck_height.ground_level: Ground-level decks in Bozeman often skip permits and railings — simplest build.
deck_height.low: Low-profile decks can use deck blocks instead of poured footings in most Gallatin County soils.
deck_height.standard: Above 30 inches, Gallatin County requires railings and typically a building permit.
deck_height.elevated: Elevated decks need engineered plans, deeper footings, and structural bracing for Montana wind loads.

material.pt_lumber: Pressure-treated is the workhorse — $2–3/LF at Kenyon Noble, needs staining every 2–3 Montana winters.
material.cedar: Cedar weathers beautifully in Montana's dry climate but costs 25–40% more than PT.
material.composite_mid: Mid-range composite handles Bozeman's freeze-thaw cycles well with zero annual maintenance.
material.composite_premium: Premium composite has the best color retention through Montana's intense UV — 25-year fade warranty.

railing.none: No railing keeps the profile clean and the cost down — works great for ground-level decks.
railing.wood: Wood railings are the most cost-effective option and match the Bozeman mountain aesthetic.
railing.metal_cable: Cable rail gives you unobstructed mountain views — popular for decks facing the Bridgers.
railing.glass: Glass panels maximize views but add significant cost — $90–150 per linear foot installed.

features.stairs: Custom stairs with wide treads and solid stringers — we build for Montana's snow and ice.
features.lighting: Low-voltage LED lighting extends your deck season into Bozeman's long summer evenings.
features.builtin_bench: Built-in benches double as railing in some configurations — saves cost and looks sharp.
features.pergola: Pergolas provide shade during Montana's intense summer sun and anchor the outdoor space.
features.hottub_pad: Hot tub pads need reinforced framing or a separate foundation — we account for the 3,000+ lb load.
features.skirting: Skirting keeps critters out and gives a finished look — lattice or solid board options.
